title: How to Actually Compare Two Commercial Leases (Without Getting Burned on the Fine Print)
excerpt: Face rent is the most visible number in a commercial lease — and usually the least important. Here's how to build a proper comparison that shows you what you'll actually pay.
author: Joe Kelley
metaTitle: How to Compare Commercial Leases in Australia | Your Office Space
metaDescription: Face rent is only one number in a commercial lease. Learn how to compare net effective rent, outgoings, incentives and make-good to find the real cost of each option.
body:
You've shortlisted two spaces. Both look right on paper. One is $450/sqm, the other $490/sqm. The cheaper one wins, right?

Not necessarily. Face rent is the least reliable number in a commercial lease comparison. The actual cost of each tenancy depends on a handful of variables that don't show up in the headline figure.

## Net effective rent vs face rent

Face rent is what's advertised. Net effective rent is what you actually pay after incentives are factored in.

Landlords often offer rent-free periods — commonly 1 month per year of lease term in the current market — to attract tenants. A 3-year lease at $490/sqm with 3 months rent-free has a lower net effective rent than a 3-year lease at $450/sqm with no incentive.

The formula: Net Effective Rent = (Face Rent × Lease Term) − (Rent-Free Months × Monthly Rent) ÷ Lease Term

Do this calculation for every option before you compare anything else.

## Outgoings — the number most tenants underestimate

Outgoings are the operating costs of the building charged back to tenants. They include:

- Council rates
- Water and sewer
- Building insurance
- Common area maintenance and cleaning
- Property management fees
- Land tax (in some leases)
- Lift maintenance, fire services, security

In a gross lease, outgoings are bundled into the rent. In a net lease, they're charged separately — and they typically add $80–$180/sqm per year on top of face rent in Australian commercial buildings.

Always get the current year's outgoings budget before comparing leases. Ask for two or three years of historical actuals to understand how they've moved.

## Rent review clauses

How your rent increases over the term matters as much as the starting figure. Commercial leases typically use one of three mechanisms:

- **Fixed percentage** — 3% or 4% annually, predictable and budgetable
- **CPI** — tracks inflation; can move in either direction
- **Market review** — rent is reset to current market at a review date; can spike significantly

A lease at $450/sqm with a market review clause in year 3 can cost more over the full term than one at $490/sqm with a fixed 3% annual increase. Model it out.

## Fit-out contribution and incentives

Landlords in a tenant-favourable market often offer fit-out contributions — cash or rent abatement to help you fit out the space. This is especially common in CBD buildings and purpose-built commercial parks.

A $50,000 fit-out contribution is real value. Factor it into your comparison as a reduction in total cost of occupation.

## Make-good exposure

We've written about make-good separately — but include it in your comparison. If Space A has a full reinstatement clause and Space B has a fair wear and tear clause, that difference could be worth $50,000–$150,000 at the end of the lease.

## How to build the comparison

For each option, calculate:
1. Net effective rent per sqm per year
2. Estimated outgoings per sqm per year
3. Total annual cost of occupation
4. Total cost over the full lease term
5. Less: any incentives or fit-out contribution
6. Plus: estimated make-good cost

That gives you a true apples-to-apples comparison.

We've built a free Lease Comparison Tool that does this automatically. You can run both options through it at yourofficespace.au/resources/lease-comparison.

Or if you want us to run the numbers with your actual documents — that's what the LeaseIntel™ review is for.
